author: @marcoviniciomorafallas1719
text:
Hello, I really enjoyed your video.

I've been thinking about a CVT and wanted to share an idea that might interest you:
Have you ever considered a CVT based on a tilted plate, similar to those used in axial piston hydraulic pumps?
These pumps vary the stroke of the pistons by changing the plate angle. By replacing the pistons with transmission arms and converting axial motion into rotation, it could be possible to create a compact, fully mechanical CVT with continuously variable ratio.

I loved your approach, and I'd like to go through the trial-and-error process you mentioned — but since I don't have the time, I thought I'd at least share the idea with you.

Best regards, and thank you for the inspiration!

Saludos desde Costa Rica. 🇨🇷

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @Forensic1Man
text: Rob, I appreciate you highlighting the importance of persistence in perfecting a project. In my antique watch and clock repair business, crafting a precisely fitting gear, arbor, or other critical part often takes multiple attempts to get it just right. Through years of working with various metals, I've honed new skills in fabricating precise small parts. Whether making large or tiny components, achieving a perfect fit sometimes requires several fabrication tries to ensure the new piece works seamlessly in place of a worn-out or broken one.  Thanks for sharing your insights—they truly resonate with craftsmen like me!

author: @Voo_Doo_Blue
text:
How about variable pitch blades on a flyball governor drive?
Seems like I've seen this before.
Basically, the blades default to maximum pitch to catch the minimum breeze to begin turning. As the wind speed picks up, the governor begins to change the propeller pitch to 'spill' more air. Basically, it makes itself a less efficient turbine, using only part of the potential power of the wind available. BUT, that means when it's working at 100% of its capability, it doesn't need any more input, so gale force winds are ignored, and only the first portion of that wind is uaed.
The old wind mills in the american Middle West did this with a spring-loaded tail. As the wind increased, the tail turned the fan sideways to the wind, which made it turn slower. If it slowed too much, the tail started to straighten back out, and the fan caught more wind. Repeat as needed.
These windmills survived tornado force winds passing near them, but not a direct tornado hit,as that promptly relocated them a few miles away, looking more like pretzels than.windmills any longer.
Farmers would tie the tails sideways to perform maintenance tasks, or if they knew there was a storm coming ahead of time, just to ease the strain on the mechanical systems.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice post mate - thanks for taking the time to share

replies.author: @Voo_Doo_Blue
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering   Robert, the fact that you responded to me blows my mind!!!
I have been watching you for years. You have an amazingly easy style of teaching. I appreciate you so much, and I know others do as well.
I was so sad when you lost your Patty. It was around the same time I lost my mother, so I was pretty gutted myself.

Please don't ever stop posting. I appreciate every one of your videos. Even the ones that don't relate directly to one of my projects have great information that I can use elsewhere.

Thanks for taking the time to respond, and have a great day! Fan #15. There from the start. 😉

author: @YouSayYouSaw
text: Sorry! I did enjoy your video. I’m a retired R&D director for an oil well drilling fluids company, so I really enjoy watching the things you come up with. I’m constantly tinkering and have used a few of your ideas along the way. You always help me think outside the box. As you mention in today’s video, sometimes looking around at what’s out there, rather than focusing on a rigid straight line view to figure out a best way to accomplish something. The various heaters you posted this winter were great projects as well. Anyway, sorry for the strange comment…hope this one is better?!😊
